elements.sent-2411-002: In this work, we investigate the existence of wormholes within the framework of Loop Quantum Cosmology, considering isotropic dark matter as the matter source.
elements.sent-2411-003: We examine three distinct density profiles and employ modified field equations along with stress-energy tensor conservation, applying suitable boundary conditions to derive traversable wormhole solutions.
elements.sent-2411-004: We confirm that each obtained solution meets the geometric requirements for wormholes and assess the regularity of these spacetimes by computing the Kretschmann scalar to ensure the absence of singularities.
elements.sent-2411-005: Through the stress-energy tensor, we analyze the conditions under which energy conditions may be violated in this model.
elements.sent-2411-006: We explore the geometry of these wormholes using embedding diagrams, and evaluate the stability of the solutions by examining the speed of sound.
elements.sent-2411-007: Lastly, we compute the amount of exotic matter necessary to sustain these structures using the Volume Integral Quantifier.
elements.sent-2411-008: General Relativity (GR) has successfully described gravitational phenomena for over a century, providing a robust framework for understanding spacetime curvature as influenced by matter and energy.
elements.sent-2411-009: This theory's predictions, from planetary orbits to black hole properties, have been verified extensively, notably in strong-field regimes with the detection of gravitational waves by LIGO and Virgo collaborations \cite{LIGOScientific:2016aoc .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Another fascinating consequence of GR is the possibility of traversable wormholes, hypothetical passages through spacetime that could connect distant points or even different universes \cite{Einstein:1935tc,Morris:1988cz,Hawking:1988ae .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Unlike black holes, which are characterized by an event horizon that prevents anything from escaping, traversable wormholes remain open, allowing for potential passage by particles and light.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, constructing stable wormhole solutions within GR typically requires exotic matter -- substances that violate energy conditions such as the Null Energy Condition (NEC) \cite{Visser:1995cc,Nandi:2004ku,Churilova:2021tgn,Konoplya:2021hsm .}
elements.sent-2411-013: This need for exotic matter has driven investigations into alternative sources and modifications of GR, particularly in theories that incorporate quantum gravitational effects, such as Loop Quantum Gravity (LQG).
elements.sent-2411-014: Loop Quantum Cosmology (LQC), a simplified model of LQG, introduces corrections to classical GR, especially at high densities, by imposing a critical density \(\rho_c\), beyond which quantum geometric effects become significant \cite{Ashtekar:2008ay .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In this sense, traversable wormholes in scenarios of LQC were built in \cite{Sengupta:2023yof,Muniz:2024jzg .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Such quantum effects hold the potential to reduce or even eliminate the need for exotic matter in sustaining wormholes, allowing other forms of matter, such as dark matter, to support their structure.
elements.sent-2411-017: The enigmatic nature of dark matter remains one of physics' greatest puzzles, thought to comprise around five-sixths of the universe's matter \cite{aghanim2020planck .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Its presence is strongly supported by astrophysical observations, such as galaxy rotation curves that indicate more mass than visible matter alone can explain \cite{zwicky1933rotverschiebung,Rubin:1970zza,persic1996,bertone2018history .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Dark matter's mass scale, spanning from cosmic structures down to \(10^{-22 \, \text{eV}\), is still undetermined, making the identification of its particle nature a priority in modern physics \cite{randall2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: One theory suggests that primordial black holes, originating shortly after the Big Bang, might serve as dark matter.
elements.sent-2411-021: Another possibility involves new particles beyond the Standard Model, like axions and Weakly Interacting Massive Particles (WIMPs), which might accumulate and annihilate in the Sun, emitting neutrinos detectable by observatories like IceCube, though no confirmation has yet been found \cite{alejandro2021,Marsh:2024ury .}
elements.sent-2411-022: It is worth noting that dark matter as a source of wormholes was investigated in several papers, in Einstein and modified gravity \cite{Xu:2020wfm,Muniz:2022eex,Mustafa:2023kqt,Radhakrishnan:2024rnm,Errehymy:2024lhl,Maurya:2024jos,Hassan:2024xyx .}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this work, we explore the existence of traversable wormholes sourced by isotropic dark matter within the framework of LQC.
elements.sent-2411-024: We investigate three cold dark matter models -- Navarro-Frenk-White (NFW), Pseudo-Isothermal (PI), and Perfect Fluid (PF) models \cite{Navarro:1995iw,Begeman:1991iy -- each with distinct density profiles that influence the stability and structure of wormhole solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-025: While in the context of General Relativity dark matter does not possess an exotic nature, it may exhibit such characteristics within certain modified gravity frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-026: Then we adopt a linear equation of state, \( p = \omega \rho \), where \(\omega\) can potentially take on negative values.
elements.sent-2411-027: Thus, by employing the LQC-modified Einstein and conservation equations with linear EoS, we derive shape and redshift functions for each dark matter model to assess whether the resulting geometries satisfy the conditions for traversable wormholes, such as the flaring-out condition and asymptotic flatness.
elements.sent-2411-028: Following, we examine the regularity conditions of the corresponding spacetimes by computing the Kretschmann scalar.
elements.sent-2411-029: We further examine energy conditions by analyzing the effective stress-energy tensor derived from the dark matter density profiles, noting that the violation of NEC may vary with the choice of model and parameters, particularly \(\rho_c\) and \(\rho_0\), the LQC critical density and central density of dark matter, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-030: Through embedding diagrams, we visually represent the wormhole shapes for each model, illustrating how changes in the parameters influence their structure.
elements.sent-2411-031: Furthermore, by calculating the Volume Integral Quantifier (VIQ), we evaluate the amount of exotic matter required to sustain each wormhole, comparing the models between themselves.
elements.sent-2411-032: The stability of these wormholes is also analyzed through sound speed calculations within the employed isotropic dark matter fluids.
elements.sent-2411-033: This work underscores, therefore, the role of quantum corrections from LQC in potentially creating stable, traversable wormholes sustained by dark matter, advancing the study of non-classical spacetimes as viable exotic structures within modified gravity theories.
elements.sent-2411-034: The structure of this paper is as follows: In Section II, we introduce the dark matter models to be used and the modified Einstein equations for general traversable wormhole solutions in the context of LQC.
elements.sent-2411-035: In Sections III, IV, and V, we derive these wormhole solutions for the three dark matter models and discuss their geometric properties.
elements.sent-2411-036: Section VI explores the embedding diagrams of the obtained wormhole solutions.
elements.sent-2411-037: In Section VII, we examine the energy conditions and calculate the necessary amount of exotic matter.
elements.sent-2411-038: In Section VIII, we analyze the stability of the solutions by investigating the sound velocity within the isotropic fluid.
elements.sent-2411-039: Finally, Section IX presents the conclusions and closes the paper.
elements.sent-2411-040: Throughout this paper, we utilize natural units with $8 \pi G = c =1$ and adopt the metric signature $(-, +, +, +).$.
elements.section-2411-002: Dark matter models and LQC-Inspired Wormholes
elements.sent-2411-041: We will work with the cold dark matter models whose density profiles can be synthesized in the following formula: \begin{equation}\label{dark matterProfiles} \rho(r)=\frac{\rho_0}{\sum_{n=0}^3 a_n (r/R_s)^n},\end{equation} where $R_s$ and $\rho_0$ are a distance scale and a density parameter, respectively, which are associated with the dark matter distribution.
elements.sent-2411-042: The coefficients $a_n$ depends on the model under analysis.
elements.sent-2411-043: Thus, we have \begin{eqnarray}\text{NFW: } a_0=0,\text{ }a_1=1,\text{ }a_2=2,\text{ }a_3=1; \label{nfwDens} \text{PI: } a_0=1,\text{ }a_1=0,\text{ }a_2=1,\text{ }a_3=0; \label{piDens} \text{PF: } a_0=a_1=a_2=0,\text{ }a_3=1,\label{pfDens}\end{eqnarray} where NFW stands for Navarro-Frenk-White, PI is for Pseudo-Isothermal, and PF denotes Perfect Fluid model.

elements.sent-2411-099: In order to better analyze the shape of the wormholes we found, we will study the embedding diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-100: These type of diagrams help us to understand the curvature of the spacetime around compact objects, such as the wormholes.
elements.sent-2411-101: To construct these diagrams, we will consider the line element that describes our wormhole models for the case $t = \text{constant}$ and considering the equatorial plane, $\theta = \pi/2$.
elements.sent-2411-102: With these simplifications, the line element is written as: \begin{equation}\label{metric2d} ds^2=\frac{dr^2}{1-\frac{b(r)}{r}}+r^2d\phi^2.\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-007: Energy Conditions and quantity of exotic matter
elements.sent-2411-112: The energy conditions are constraints imposed on the components of a given stress-energy tensor to ensure its physical viability.
elements.sent-2411-113: In the case of wormholes, the violation of certain energy conditions is essential to keep the wormhole's throat traversable.
elements.sent-2411-114: To our case, let's consider the components of the stress-energy tensor given by \eqref{energy_tensor .}
elements.sent-2411-115: The energy conditions are given by the inequalities: \begin{eqnarray} NEC=WEC_{1}=SEC_{1} \Longleftrightarrow \rho_e+p_{e}\geq 0,\label{Econd1} SEC_2 \Longleftrightarrow\rho_e+3p_e\geq 0,\label{Econd2} DEC_{1} \Longleftrightarrow \rho_e-|p_{e}|\geq 0 \Longleftrightarrow (\rho_e+p_{e}\geq 0) \hbox{ and } (\rho_e-p_{e}\geq 0),\label{Econd3} DEC_2=WEC_2 \Longleftrightarrow\rho_e\geq 0,\label{Econd4}\end{eqnarray} where the energy conditions are the null (NEC), weak (WEC), strong (SEC), and dominant (DEC).
elements.sent-2411-116: We see that the NEC is embedded within the other conditions.
elements.sent-2411-117: Thus, if the NEC is violated, the other conditions will also be violated.
elements.sent-2411-118: However, the reverse is not necessarily true, as the other conditions also impose additional inequalities that do not appear in the NEC.
elements.sent-2411-119: Now let's analyze the conditions for each dark matter model.
elements.sent-2411-120: In this section, we will also analyze the quantity of exotic matter necessary to maintain the wormhole by calculating the Volume Integral Quantifier (VIQ), defined by \cite{Nandi:2004 \begin{equation}\label{viq} \mathcal{I}_v=\int_{r_0}^r 4\pi x^2 (\rho_e+p_e)dx,\end{equation} where $r\to \infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-121: We will perform this calculation for the three dark matter models considered up to this point.

elements.sent-2411-169: In this section we evaluate the stability of the obtained wormhole solutions through the analysis of the radial sound velocity within the dark matter fluid \cite{Capozziello:2022zoz .}
elements.sent-2411-170: This quantity, denoted as $(v_s)^2$, can be calculated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{vsound} (v_s)^2=\frac{d p_e}{d\rho_e}=\frac{d p_e/dr}{d\rho_e/dr}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-171: Wormhole stability is ensured if \(v_s^2 > 0\), with the condition \(v_s < 1\) required to maintain subluminal velocities.
elements.sent-2411-172: Our analysis focuses on the throat at \(r = r_0\).
elements.sent-2411-173: In left panel of Figure \ref{fig_vsq , we illustrate the parameter space \((\rho_c, \omega)\) for various values of the critical dark matter density \(\rho_0\), with other parameters held constant.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Although the plot describes the stability and physicality of the NFW model, the behavior is similar in the other dark matter models.
elements.sent-2411-175: Notably, a lower concentration of \(\rho_0\) expands (reduces) the regions where stability and physical viability are achieved, particularly for \(\omega > 0\) ( \(\omega < 0\) ).
elements.sent-2411-176: Conversely, higher values of \(\rho_0\) reduce (expand) these regions.
elements.sent-2411-177: We also observe that as the solutions become classical ({\it i.e. , non-quantum, \(\rho_c \to \infty\)), the dark matter-sourced wormhole solutions tend to the stability, particularly for \(\omega \geq 0\).}
elements.sent-2411-178: In the right panel of the same figure, we compare the stability and physicality regions across the parameter spaces of the three models.
elements.sent-2411-179: Notably, the PI model exhibits the largest parameter space for \(\omega > 0\), while the PF model shows the largest region for \(\omega < 0\).
elements.sent-2411-180: In other words, the PF model favors an exotic, quantum nature of dark matter in scenarios where non-classical effects ($\rho_c\to 0$) become more significant.
elements.sent-2411-181: In this paper, we obtain wormhole solutions in the context of LQC.
elements.sent-2411-182: As a source of matter, we chose three different dark matter profiles that are well-known and studied in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-183: To obtain these solutions, we solved the field equations of LQC and the conservation equation for the energy-momentum tensor, with the appropriate boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-184: It was also necessary to choose an equation of state, for which we adopted $p = \omega \rho$.
elements.sent-2411-185: So that, we obtained the appropriate expressions for the shape function, $b(r)$, and the redshift function, $\Phi(r)$.
elements.sent-2411-186: Even though it was obtained through the boundary conditions for a wormhole, it was still necessary to verify whether the shape function satisfied certain criteria.

elements.sent-2411-210: In this work, we investigate the existence of wormholes within the framework of Loop Quantum Cosmology, using isotropic dark matter as the source.
elements.sent-2411-211: We analyze three distinct density profiles and solve the modified gravity field equations alongside the stress-energy tensor conservation, applying appropriate boundary conditions to obtain traversable wormhole solutions.
elements.sent-2411-212: Each solution is shown to satisfy the geometric criteria for wormholes, and their regularity is verified by computing the Kretschmann scalar to ensure the absence of singularities under determined conditions.
elements.sent-2411-213: Additionally, we examine the stress-energy tensor to identify scenarios in which energy conditions are violated within this model.
elements.sent-2411-214: The wormhole geometry is further explored through embedding diagrams, and the amount of exotic matter required to sustain these structures is computed using the Volume Integral Quantifier.
elements.sent-2411-215: The enigmatic nature of dark matter remains one of physics' greatest puzzles, thought to comprise around five-sixths of the universe's matter \cite{Planck:2018vyg .}
elements.sent-2411-216: Its presence is strongly supported by astrophysical observations, such as galaxy rotation curves that indicate more mass than visible matter alone can explain \cite{Zwicky:1933gu,Rubin:1970zza,Persic:1995ru,Bertone:2016nfn .}
elements.sent-2411-217: Another possibility involves new particles beyond the Standard Model, like axions and Weakly Interacting Massive Particles (WIMPs), which might accumulate and annihilate in the Sun, emitting neutrinos detectable by observatories like IceCube, though no confirmation has yet been found \cite{Arguelles:2019ouk,Marsh:2024ury .}
elements.sent-2411-218: There are also proposals to detect dark matter in the vicinity of the Sun using satellites orbiting our star \cite{Tsai:2021lly,Souza:2024ltj .}
elements.sent-2411-219: This work underscores, therefore, the role of quantum corrections from LQC in potentially creating traversable wormholes sustained by dark matter, advancing the study of non-classical spacetimes as viable exotic structures within modified gravity theories.
elements.sent-2411-220: Finally, Section VIII presents the conclusions and closes the paper.
